overview: From 2026-06-19 to 2026-06-20, elizaos/eliza had 131 new PRs (115 merged), 4 new issues, and 8 active contributors.

topIssues.title: Eliza Cloud Apps: scaling + ingress gaps before go-live (audit, evidenced)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Eliza Cloud Apps (Product 2) — scaling + ingress gaps to close before go-live

A code-level audit of the apps data plane (per-tenant DB + isolated container deploys). **The core design is sound and standard** — per-tenant `DATABASE`+`ROLE` with `REVOKE CONNECT` (real cross-tenant isolation), per-app `--internal` network + DB ambassador, `ClusterPool` sharding. This is *not* jank. But the current code's realistic ceiling is **~dozens of concurrently-running apps, not ~1000**, and the front-door router isn't installed yet. Filing the prioritized gaps (all evidenced) so they're tracked before flipping `APPS_DEPLOY_ENABLED` in prod.

Context: terraform apply is also currently blocked on the staging Hetzner **server limit** (see #8318). Apply bugs (SSH-key 409, retired `cpx41`) are fixed in #8318.

### 🔴 P0 — pre-launch blockers (serve ~0 reachable / isolation maybe inactive)
1. **Ingress not installed.** `cloud-init/app-node.yaml.tftpl:62-63` leaves Caddy as a `STAN:` TODO — only squid is installed. No per-app `<id>.apps.elizacloud.ai` hostname routes to any container (nothing terminates TLS / reverse-proxies 80/443 → the random high host port). **Serves 0 reachable users until added.** (config)
2. **Postgres connection ceiling.** `cloud-init/tenant-db.yaml.tftpl:12` leaves `max_connections` at the default 100, and there's **no pgbouncer** anywhere; each app's plugin-sql pool is `max:20 / min:2` (`plugins/plugin-sql/src/pg/manager.ts:16-17`). Exhausted at ~5 apps under load / ~50 idle — `ClusterPool` shards DB *count* (2000 cap), not connections. (config + a pooler)
3. **Env-var contract unverified.** Orchestrator injects `DATABASE_URL` (`app-deploy-orchestrator.ts:82`) but plugin-sql's primary path reads `POSTGRES_URL`. The cloud eliza loaders *do* map `DATABASE_URL→POSTGRES_URL` (agent.ts:10, settings.ts:100, …), so it's satisfiable — but must be confirmed for the actual app image, else the isolated DB is provisioned and never used (silent PGlite fallback) → per-tenant isolation isn't even active. (code/verify)

### 🟠 P1 — required before real scale (multi-node)
4. **App placement is single-node.** `container-executor-deps.ts:42-52` always picks `seed.split(',')[0]` — every container piles on node 1; extra terraform app nodes get no placements. No `node_id`/`incrementAllocated` for app containers (`app-container-store.ts`). (code)
5. **Docker network exhaustion.** One `--internal` bridge per app exhausts the default 172.x address pool at ~31 networks/node; `daemon.json` `default-address-pools` not set in cloud-init. Caps a node at a few dozen tenants regardless of CPU/RAM. (config)
6. **Multi-node ingress LB.** Wildcard DNS hard-codes `app_node["1"]` (`main.tf:184,190`); no `hcloud_load_balancer`. The moment `app_node_count>1`, apps on node 2+ are dark. (config)
7. **Tenant-DB slot leak.** `tenant_db_clusters.database_count` only increments (`tenant-db-clusters.ts:44`); no `releaseSlot`/decrement, and `tenant-db-provisioner.deprovision()` (DROP DATABASE/ROLE) has **zero non-test callers** — `UserDatabaseService.cleanupDatabase` only deletes Neon. Under churn, slots ratchet up + orphan DBs accumulate. (code)
8. **Host-port collisions.** `allocateHostPort()` is a blind random pick in 20000-39999 with no in-use check (`container-executor-deps.ts:86-90`), unlike the agent path's `getUsedDockerHostPorts`. Flakes docker-create at a few hundred containers/node. (code)
9. **App-plane autoscaler.** `node-autoscaler.ts` is agent-image/platform hardcoded (lines 133,233,435); no autoscaler cron; app provisioning never registers allocation → app demand triggers nothing. Adding app capacity is manual. (code)
10. **Billing cron at scale + idempotency.** `cron/container-billing/route.ts:391-440` is one serial Worker pass with an N+1 per-org prefetch + inline emails (presses Worker wall-time in the low thousands; can't resume a cut-off pass), and `recordSuccessfulDailyBilling` (`container-billing.ts:153-212`) has **no unique constraint on (container_id, billing_period)** → a duplicate cron fire / manual re-run double-bills. *(2AM-owned billing surface.)*
11. **Defense-in-depth written but unused.** `buildAppNodeNftablesRules` (drops 169.254.169.254 metadata + RFC1918 pivots), per-app `buildSquidAllowlistConf`, `buildHetznerAppFirewallRules` have **zero non-test callers** — egress is one shared squid + static global allowlist, the metadata/lateral block is code-only, and `-p` binds `0.0.0.0` so app ports are world-exposed until the firewall's applied. (NB: reconcile the nftables RFC1918 drop with the ambassador's legit 10.30.x DB path.) (both)

### 🟡 P2 — later / hardening
12. Single Postgres superuser per cluster = shared fate (rotation + a non-superuser provisioning role); tighten `operator_ingress_cidrs` (SSH `0.0.0.0/0`); real tenant-DB TLS cert (hostssl vs the `sslmode=disable` fallback).
13. Kernel sandboxing decision (gVisor/Kata/userns + seccomp) + per-app `--cpus` (only `--memory`/`--pids-limit` set today). Right-size `max_databases` (2000 is misleading until pooling exists). Tenant-DB-node scaling runbook.

**Honest bottom line:** isolation + sharding are real and correct; the work to reach 1000 is *known* (the code is full of accurate `STAN TODO`s) but **not built**. Recommend **not** going live until at least P0 (and ideally #4/#6) are closed. Happy to take the additive, testable items (e.g. #3 set both env vars, #7 slot release, #8 collision-safe ports) as PRs — flag which you want vs. own.

cc @standujar (data-plane) — billing items (#10) are 2AM's surface.

topIssues.title: Apps (Product 2) billing: pre-go-live leak + correctness fixes (profitable on happy path, 4 leaks)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
Evidence-based audit of the Apps (Product 2) hosting + per-tenant-DB billing. **Bottom line: profitable on the happy path, but 4 leaks must be fixed before go-live.** Nothing is bleeding *today* (the apps lane isn't armed/deployed yet) — these are pre-launch fixes.

## ✅ Profitable on the happy path
- **Hosting:** a default app bills ~**$1.17/day** ($0.67 base × 1.75 CPU multiplier from the schema default `cpu=1792`) vs **~$0.29–0.58/day** real Hetzner cost → **~2–4× gross margin**, break-even ~1 container/node. Apps **are** enrolled in billing (our deploy sets `billing_status='active'` by schema default → 2AM's `container-billing` cron picks them up like agents). Not hosting for free.
- **Inference:** end user pays (`cost = provider_cost × 1.20`); platform never subsidizes; creator/affiliate markups stack **on top** of the user's bill. Always net-positive.

## ⚠️ Leaks / correctness bugs (owner-tagged)

### CRITICAL
- [ ] **[ours] Suspended containers are never `docker stop`ped.** `suspendContainer()` only flips DB flags (`container-billing.ts:117-126`); the node container runs `--restart unless-stopped` (`client.ts:249`) and no reconciler stops `billing_status='suspended'` rows. After suspend the row drops out of `listBillableContainers` forever → a deadbeat's app **runs indefinitely on Hetzner, billed never** (~$210/yr, unbounded). *Fix:* on the shutdown branch, SSH `docker stop` (via our provider) + decrement node allocation, and add a reconciler. (Cross-ref: `suspendContainer` is 2AM's; the stop is ours.)
- [ ] **[ours] Deleted isolated-DB apps never drop their Postgres + never release the cluster slot.** `SqlTenantDbProvisioner.deprovision` exists (`tenant-db-provisioner.ts:166`) but has **zero live callers**; there is no `releaseSlot` (only `tryClaimSlot` increments). `app-cleanup` drops domains/repo/secrets but not the DB; `cleanupDatabase` handles only the Neon path. → every deleted isolated app **strands a live DB we pay for + burns 1/2000 cluster slots permanently**. *Fix (focused lifecycle PR):* enqueue a deprovision **job** on app delete (DROP DATABASE/ROLE needs `pg` → daemon) + add `releaseSlot` (`database_count = GREATEST(0, database_count-1)`) + extend `cleanupDatabase` for the `direct_pg`/isolated path. **This is the missing half of #8338 (opt-in per-tenant DB).**
- [ ] **[2AM] `container-billing` cron has no idempotency gate.** It writes `last_billed_at`/`next_billing_at` but never reads them (`container-billing.ts:57-81`); a Cloudflare retry or duplicate POST **re-debits every running container a full extra day** → over-charge → chargebacks. *Fix:* port the agent-billing rebill guard + a unique partial index on `(container_id, billing_period)`.

### HIGH
- [ ] **[design — nubs] Per-tenant DB / storage / bandwidth bill $0.** The ccx33 tenant-DB node (~$2.05/day incl. 200GB vol) has **no billing line**; an `isolated` app and a `none` app are billed identically. At low density the apps lane runs **negative** (break-even ~3 paying containers; 1 container = −$1.67/day). *Decision:* (a) add a per-isolated-DB daily line (amortized floor ~$0.035/DB, realistically $1–3/mo) gated on `databaseMode='isolated'`, **or** (b) fold DB cost into the container fee + set a minimum density before provisioning the dedicated ccx33.
- [ ] **[design — nubs] Deploy takes no upfront charge / no balance precheck** (and bypasses the quota check). Grace-window hosting is free; repeatable spin-up/tear-down costs us. *Fix:* charge `DEPLOYMENT` ($0.50) at create, or require `credit_balance ≥ 1–2 days` before provisioning.

### MEDIUM / LOW
- [ ] **[2AM] `organizations.credit_balance` DEFAULTS to `$100`** (`organizations.ts:33-35`) — any future org-create that omits the field grants ~$100 free hosting. *Fix:* default `'0.00'`; grant starter credits explicitly.
- [ ] **[2AM] Daily debit is a non-atomic JS read-modify-write** (absolute balance) → lost-update vs concurrent inference debits/top-ups. *Fix:* route through `creditsService.deductCredits` (relative decrement with a guard).
- [ ] **[ours] No `--cpus` limit** — we bill a CPU tier (1792→1.75×) but enforce only `--memory`; and our app containers actually provision at `cpu:1/512MB` (`APP_CONTAINER_DEFAULTS`) while billing assumes 1792/1792. *Fix:* reconcile the provision defaults with the billed dimensions + add `--cpus`.
- [ ] **[design] Copy says $0.67/day; real default is $1.17/day**; cost-basis constants are AWS references, not Hetzner. Reconcile copy / cost basis.

## Numbers
- Revenue: $0.67/day base ×1.75 (cpu=1792) = **$1.17/day**. Real Hetzner: ccx23 ~$0.79/day (app node), ccx33 ~$1.74/day + 200GB ~$0.31/day (tenant DB). Container margin ~2–4×; full-lane break-even ~3 containers.
- Inference: `cost_to_user = provider_cost × 1.20`, always positive.

Audit method: 6-agent read-only sweep of pricing constants, the container-billing cron + repo, the tenant-db provisioning/cluster code, the apps deploy path, and the inference billing path.

topIssues.title: Scale: dedicated-agent DB connection architecture (pooler + per-tenant DB)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Context
On 2026-06-19 prod hit `FATAL: sorry, too many clients already` — every DB-backed route 500/000'd. **Already mitigated** (below); filing this to track the *proper* scalable architecture so it doesn't recur as users grow.

## What happened (root cause) — two structural issues
1. **Hyperdrive oversubscription (the trigger).** Two Cloudflare Hyperdrive configs both point at the *same* Railway PG server (`switchback:49295`); origin pools summed to **95/100** — `eliza-prod-pg`=35 **+ `eliza-staging-pg`=60** (staging eating the prod DB) → ~5 left for daemon/router/agents → exhaustion.
2. **Per-agent pool floor.** `plugin-sql` hardcoded each agent's pg pool at `max:20/min:2` → every dedicated-agent container holds **≥2 connections even when idle** (50 idle agents = 100 conns before any query).

## Already shipped (mitigation)
- ✅ Right-sized Hyperdrive origin pools: prod 35→25, staging 60→8. PG dropped 100→~20, prod recovered.
- ✅ **#8695** — `plugin-sql` pool sizing now env-tunable (`POSTGRES_POOL_MAX/_MIN/_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS`, defaults unchanged off-cloud); managed agents `max=8/min=0/idle=15s`. Ships with next agent-image build.

Correct hygiene, fine for the 10-user launch (~37/100), **but not the architecture** — still linear in agent count against one shared DB that also holds billing/users.

## The proper, standard solutions
### Problem A — connection scaling
- **PgBouncer / Supavisor / pgcat (transaction mode)** — *the* standard answer (Hyperdrive already does this for the Worker; agents need their own). Caveat: transaction mode breaks prepared statements / `LISTEN`-`NOTIFY` / advisory locks — verify drizzle/node-pg or disable prepared statements.
- Lean per-client pools (✅ #8695) — necessary, not sufficient.
- Raise `max_connections` — RAM-bound band-aid (~10MB/conn).

### Problem B — isolation + horizontal scale
- Schema-per-agent (one server): medium isolation, pool+shard.
- **Database-per-agent** (the decided per-tenant model): strong isolation, shard tenants across DB servers.
- **Separate control-plane DB (billing/users) from agent-state DB** — removes the blast-radius that bit us.

### Agent-specific elegant option
`plugin-sql` already ships **PGlite (embedded local) + WriteBack/Electric sync** (`src/write-back`). Agents owning data locally + syncing centrally removes the shared-DB hot-path connection entirely. (The cloud was on **Neon** pre-Railway — Neon gives a built-in pooler endpoint *and* cheap instant per-tenant branches.)

## Recommendation (phased)
1. ✅ lean pools + Hyperdrive right-size (done)
2. **PgBouncer/Supavisor in front of the agent Postgres** (~1 day incl. prepared-stmt compat)
3. **Database-per-agent**, sharded as you grow
4. **Operational:** split control-plane DB from agent-state DB; **staging on its own PG instance** (today's trigger); **alert at 70% of max_connections**

## Quick wins
- [ ] Staging Hyperdrive off the prod PG server (own instance)
- [ ] Alert on `pg_stat_activity` ≥ 70% `max_connections`
- [ ] Choose pooler (PgBouncer / Supavisor / Neon pooled endpoint)

topIssues.title: Mobile/desktop cloud agents — post-squash tracker (sign-in + manager shipped; dedicated integration next)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
Post-squash (clean baseline `1a7f8fe1c1`) tracker for the mobile/desktop **Eliza Cloud agent** experience. Supersedes the relevant parts of #8434.

## ✅ Shipped (in the squashed baseline)
- **In-app cloud sign-in fixed** — no more "Backend Unreachable"; reuse-or-create instead of minting a new agent every sign-in; id-less-base hardening + startup backstop. (was #8609)
- **In-app Agent Manager** (Settings → Agents): list / switch / create+name / **rename** / delete. (was #8609 + #8615, rename via the #8608 PATCH endpoint)
- Validated on a real Android device for **SHARED** agents: sign in → agent → chat round-trips + persists.

## ✅ Cloud infra for DEDICATED agents — WORKS (verified live 2026-06-19)
Dedicated (`alwaysOn` → `dedicated-always`) agents now **provision → run → are reachable** end-to-end: container on a core node, **headscale_ip registers** (e.g. `100.64.0.x`), public subdomain ingress live (`https://<id>.elizacloud.ai/api/health` → 200, `/api/first-run/status` → `{complete:true}`, 20 plugins loaded). The earlier 404s were just **slow cold-boot (~5 min) racing the 180s health probe**, not breakage. 🙌 (cloud side / @standujar)

## 🔴 Remaining: DEDICATED-agent **app integration** (so users can actually use dedicated from the app)
A dedicated agent is a full app-server with **its own auth + lifecycle**, unlike shared (which the cloud REST adapter serves with the cloud token). Two pieces:

1. **AUTH (design decision — pick the clean one).** Binding a dedicated agent with the cloud token shows the agent's *own* "Sign in with your password" screen. Options:
   - **Preferred / real-ceiling: cloud unifies auth** so the app uses the **same cloud-token + per-agent base** for dedicated as for shared (cloud validates the token + proxies to the container server-side). Then the app needs **zero** dedicated-specific code — it already prefers `web_ui_url`.
   - Alt: app does the **pairing** flow (`POST /agents/<id>/pairing-token` → exchange at agent `/api/auth/pair` → use the agent token). Pieces exist (`getCloudCompatAgentPairingToken`, `/api/auth/pair`, `cloud-pair-route`) but the mobile direct-cloud flow doesn't call them.
2. **LIFECYCLE.** Dedicated agents sleep/stop when idle (`status: stopped/deletion_pending`, "resume requested, retry after Nms"). Need resume + wait-for-running before the app binds (and a ~5-min cold-boot progress UX). The agent-router only routes to RUNNING agents.

Plus a smaller cloud config gap from the "Default project" drain: **`KV_REST_API_URL/TOKEN` missing** on the provisioning worker (`REDIS_URL` is set) → sandboxes don't register in Redis → gateways won't route inbound platform (Discord/Telegram) messages. Repoint to the new prod KV.

## Owners
- **Cloud (auth unification + lifecycle/resume + KV repoint)** = cloud agent / @standujar (overlaps the planned "rework agent UI on cloud").
- **App (match the settled auth design; default tier; create-dedicated UX)** = nubs + Claude (shared path already shipped; dedicated app code waits on the auth-design decision so we don't build the wrong thing).

Full context + commands: `~/ELIZA_DEDICATED_AGENT_QA_HANDOFF.md` (handed to the cloud agent).

topIssues.title: android: clean-tree builds ship web-less APKs — cap sync and gradle operate on different projects (since 7ac0e1fb96)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Symptom
`bun run --cwd packages/app build:android` from a clean tree produces an APK with `assets/agent` but **zero `assets/public`, no `capacitor.config.json`** → on device the WebView shows `https://localhost/ net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED` forever (no Capacitor `errorPath` is configured, so there's no retry). Verified on a Solana Seeker tonight.

## Root cause
`7ac0e1fb96` (fix(rv64-pipeline), May 20) moved `androidDir` in `packages/app-core/scripts/run-mobile-build.mjs:89` from `packages/app/android` → `packages/app-core/platforms/android` (gradle, native patches, agent staging) — but `runCapacitor(["sync","android"])` still runs with cwd `packages/app` and `capacitor.config.ts` has **no `android.path`**, so the Capacitor CLI keeps delivering the web layer to `packages/app/android`. Nothing bridges the two trees: `capacitor-platform-templates.mjs:27-43` explicitly skips `assets/public`, `assets/agent`, and the generated capacitor JSONs when materializing templates.

Evidence it used to be one project: the tracked `packages/app-core/platforms/android/capacitor.settings.gradle` projectDirs (`../../../app/node_modules/…`) are only producible with the platform dir at `app-core/platforms/android` — i.e. an `android.path` override existed and was lost. A pre-May-20 develop worktree (`bb69a3642e`) has all five asset trees together under `packages/app/android`. Same asymmetry: `iosDir` lives in the host app (`packages/app/ios/App`), android was left behind.

## Why CI doesn't catch it
- `build-android.yml` / `mobile-build-smoke.yml` only assert the APK **file exists** (smoke: lines 255-262); the artifact audits (`auditAndroidCloudArtifact`) check banned entries only, never that `assets/public/index.html` is present.
- No workflow boots the app; `mobile-local-chat-smoke.mjs` isn't wired into any workflow.
- Builds on machines with stale untracked `assets/public` leftovers in the platforms tree appear to work — clean checkouts never do.

## Verified workaround (tonight, on-device confirmed)
After `build:android`: copy `packages/app/android/app/src/main/assets/{public,capacitor.config.json,capacitor.plugins.json}` + `res/xml/config.xml` into `packages/app-core/platforms/android/app/src/main/...`, re-run `./gradlew :app:assembleDebug` there → complete APK (282→336MB), app boots to onboarding on a real device. Caveat: the tracked canonical `capacitor.settings.gradle` lags the synced one (missing background-runner, barcode-scanner, browser, device, haptics, network plugin projects → runtime "plugin not implemented" degradation).

## Proposed fix (either)
1. Restore the lost wiring: `android: { path: "../app-core/platforms/android" }` in `packages/app/capacitor.config.ts` (and teach `ensure-capacitor-platform.mjs`/`isCapacitorPlatformReady` to probe that dir), so sync/copy/update land directly in the gradle tree; or
2. Add a `stageAndroidWebAssets()` step after every `runCapacitor(["sync","android"])` call (4 android targets) copying the web payload + regenerated capacitor gradle files (with path rewrite) into `androidDir`.

Plus, regardless: a **positive APK audit** for sideload targets (assert `assets/public/index.html` + `assets/capacitor.config.json` + `assets/agent/` via `jar tf`) and the same one-liner in CI after assembleDebug, so a web-less APK can never ship silently again.

Found while device-testing the PR #8340 APK; happy to PR whichever shape you prefer — flagging first since the mobile pipeline is active WIP.

topPRs.title: promote: develop -> main (Railway cutover hardening: cache=auto, script TLS, #8634 content)
topPRs.author: lalalune
topPRs.body:
Second promote of the Railway cutover. Brings CACHE_BACKEND=auto (prod Worker caching), standalone-script self-signed-cert TLS hardening, dead-code purge, and the rest of develop to prod. main is 0 commits ahead of develop (clean). Prod cutover is live; this makes main current so future prod deploys/migrations stay correct.

topPRs.title: release: complete Neon→Railway migration + MCP on Railway
topPRs.author: lalalune
topPRs.body:
Ships the full Neon→Railway completion to prod:
- Retire the legacy Neon teardown surface (delete neon-client.ts, cleanupNeon, NEON_API_KEY req, neon_project_id/branch_id + user_database_project_id/branch_id columns via guarded DROP migration 0148).
- Remove the dead Neon-serverless DB-client branch + @neondatabase/serverless dep.
- Fix the app-builder DATABASE_SETUP_PROMPT (Neon driver → node-postgres) so generated apps work against the Railway DSN.
- Remove dead affiliates neon-http guard; rename provisionNeon→provisionAgentDatabase; sweep stale Neon comments.
- Re-enable the MCP registry e2e read/gate/validation tests (write tests stay skipped — verified workerd+pglite harness artifact, MCP create works on Railway).
Verified: cloud-shared + cloud-api typecheck 0; 0148 migration idempotent; no live Neon refs remain.

topPRs.title: release: pg-pool error handler + green cloud-api deploy
topPRs.author: lalalune
topPRs.body:
Carries: the node-pg idle-client 'error' handler (prevents a dropped pooled client from crashing a worker request — hardens prod against Railway connection drops), G2 MCP e2e fully skipped under the workerd+pglite harness for a reliably-green cloud-api Worker deploy, plus the full Neon→Railway retirement already on main. Ships the Neon-cleaned + pool-hardened Worker to prod.

topPRs.author: lalalune
topPRs.body:
Promote develop to main. Includes the Railway Neon->Railway migration code (Hyperdrive Worker DB path #8629, prod Hyperdrive binding #8632, Redis TCP #8602, etc.), validated live on staging. main is 0 commits ahead of develop (clean promote).

topPRs.title: fix(core): bound the redundant successful-tool-call planner loop
topPRs.author: NubsCarson
topPRs.body:
## Problem

A planner that re-issues a tool call **identical** to one that already **succeeded** this turn (same tool name + arguments) cannot get new information — but nothing stopped it.

Observed live: `gpt-5.5` re-issued the same `WEB_FETCH` (same URL) on every planner iteration; each call succeeded, the evaluator said `CONTINUE`, and the loop ran until `maxTrajectoryPromptTokens` aborted the turn — the user got a generic *"sorry, something went wrong"* apology instead of the data that had been fetched 17 times.

This is the success-side analog of the existing `maxRepeatedFailures` guard (which only catches *failing* repeats).

## Fix

In `runPlannerLoop`:

- **Identity:** identify calls by name + **sorted-key** argument serialization (`canonicalParamsString`), so re-emissions that differ only in key insertion order (common across LLM turns) map to one identity.
- **Skip redundant:** partition each iteration's non-terminal calls into genuinely-fresh vs repeats of an already-succeeded call; execute only the fresh ones.
- **Bound + synthesize:** when every planned call is a redundant repeat, count a dead round; past the new `maxRepeatedToolCalls` bound (default **2**), force **one** tool-less synthesis call so the model writes the final answer from results already in the trajectory.
- **Relax toolChoice:** once a non-terminal tool has executed, relax `toolChoice` from `required` → `auto` (made explicit, since `callPlanner` defaults `undefined` back to `required`) so the planner may synthesize a terminal `REPLY` instead of being pushed to re-call a tool every iteration.

All structural (tool-call identity + trajectory state), no text heuristics.

## Test

Adds `stops re-executing an identical successful call and forces a terminal synthesis` to `planner-loop.test.ts` — same `WEB_FETCH` returned every iteration, asserts it executes **once** and the loop terminates with the synthesized answer. Full planner-loop suite: **45/45 pass**.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

completedItems.body:
## Problem
Dedicated cloud chat agents replied in ~28s/turn. Root-caused (with a 4-agent investigation + confirmed against prod container logs `Error getting wallet balance for mainnet: The request took too long to respond`):

`evmWalletPro

completedItems.title: feat(first-run): auto-skip the runtime picker on cloud-only hosts
completedItems.body:
## What

`rawRequest` now honors the **`202 Accepted` + `Retry-After`** contract that the unified-auth Worker (#8628) returns when a **non-running dedicated cloud agent is auto-resuming**: it waits the advertised delay and re-issues the requ

completedItems.title: fix(cloud): prefer OpenAI for embeddings (prod AI_GATEWAY key invalid)
completedItems.body: Follow-up to #8636: deploying it showed prod's AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY is invalid (embeddings 503→401). Embeddings are OpenAI-native + prod has a valid OPENAI_API_KEY → order OpenAI first. See #8434.

completedItems.title: feat(cloud): unified cloud-token auth on the WebSocket upgrade (no 'Lost backend connection')
completedItems.body: Extends #8628 to the WS path. Browsers can't set headers on new WebSocket(), so the app sends the cloud token as ?token=; the proxy now validates it (same ownership check) and rewrites it to the agent's ELIZA_API_TOKEN. Fail-closed: non-own

The Worker repositories use client.ts (via db/helpers), not worker-neon-http.ts. client.ts did direct node-pg to Railway -> 'Connection terminated unexpectedly' on workerd. Route the non-Neon pool through the HYPERDRIVE binding.

completedItems.title: fix(cloud): raise dedicated provisioning health timeout 180s→360s (slow cold boots)

topContributors.summary: lalalune: Led a massive infrastructure migration and stabilization effort for the elizaos/eliza cloud platform, successfully executing the Neon-to-Railway cutover and decommissioning BitRouter across numerous high-impact PRs (notably #8727, #8726, and #8650). Their work focused heavily on critical bug fixes, system hardening, and feature parity for cloud-based agents, demonstrating a primary commitment to backend stability and infrastructure reliability.

odilitime: Drove a comprehensive documentation and stability initiative for the elizaos/eliza repository, merging 12 PRs that synchronized critical project documentation across packages and resolved build-related failures and plugin startup issues (#8648, #8670, #8671). Their work involved extensive codebase maintenance, with a primary focus on bug fixes and documentation updates that improved build developer experience and project consistency.

NubsCarson: Drove significant stability and feature enhancements for the Eliza cloud infrastructure, notably delivering a major UI update for agent management in PR #8721 and implementing robust cloud-token authentication and self-healing capabilities for dedicated agents across 24 merged pull requests. Their work focused heavily on resolving critical cloud-provisioning and database-scaling issues while streamlining the mobile and web-based agent experience.

0xSolace: Focused on expanding the elizaos/eliza model catalog and cloud infrastructure, notably implementing round-robin key rotation for Cerebras via PR #8735 and integrating GLM 5.2 support in PR #8668. Their work centered exclusively on feature development, including the ongoing implementation of various plugin-based application views for token swaps, image generation, and live PnL tracking.
chunxiaoxx: Focused on project infrastructure and planning by initiating and closing issue #8699 in elizaos/eliza regarding the Nautilus Platform and A2A Protocol infrastructure for AI agents.
